description: This paper introduces MULAN (Multivariate Learned Adaptive Noise), a method that learns the diffusion process in diffusion models rather than using a fixed noise schedule. The key technical innovation is a multivariate noise schedule that applies different noise rates to each pixel and adapts based on input context through auxiliary latent variables, which the authors show makes the ELBO objective non-invariant to the noise process choice. By jointly optimizing both the forward diffusion process and reverse denoising model, MULAN achieves state-of-the-art density estimation on CIFAR-10 (2.55 BPD) and ImageNet-32 (3.67 BPD). The method also reduces training time by 50% compared to previous approaches while maintaining or improving sample quality.

title: How much do language models memorize?
author: John X. Morris et al
description: This paper introduces a novel method for quantifying language model memorization by separating it into unintended memorization (dataset-specific information) and generalization (learned patterns about the data-generating process). The authors use Kolmogorov complexity approximated through compression rates to measure memorization at the instance level, estimating that GPT-family models have a capacity of approximately 3.6 bits-per-parameter. Through experiments on both synthetic random data and real text, they demonstrate that models memorize until reaching capacity, after which they begin generalizing—a transition point that coincides with the double descent phenomenon. The work produces scaling laws relating model capacity and dataset size to membership inference success, predicting that modern large language models trained on massive datasets are largely immune to membership inference attacks on average datapoints.

title: Diffusion Language Models are Provably Optimal Parallel Samplers
author: Haozhe Jiang et al
description: This paper establishes a rigorous theoretical foundation for diffusion language models (DLMs) as optimal parallel samplers by using circuit complexity theory, where circuit depth and width abstract sequential steps and memory requirements. The authors prove that DLMs with chain-of-thought (CoT) can simulate any parallel sampling algorithm using the minimum number of sequential steps (matching circuit depth), while autoregressive models require steps linear in circuit size. They demonstrate that enabling remasking (re-masking unmasked tokens) or revision (changing unmasked tokens) allows DLMs to also achieve optimal space complexity (matching circuit width), and prove these mechanisms strictly expand expressivity through a separation result on sampling zero-parity strings. The methodology frames DLMs as boolean circuits operating on encoded token sequences, formally modeling the predictor, masking policies, and generation process to derive time and space complexity bounds.

title: SpecDiff-2: Scaling Diffusion Drafter Alignment for Faster Speculative Decoding
author: Jameson Sandler et al
description: This paper introduces SpecDiff-2, which addresses two fundamental bottlenecks in speculative decoding by using discrete diffusion models as non-autoregressive drafters and developing novel alignment techniques. The key methodological contributions are: (1) Streak-distillation, a train-time alignment objective that directly optimizes the expected number of accepted tokens across the full draft window by maximizing the product of position-wise acceptance probabilities computed along verifier-generated trajectories. (2) Self-selection acceptance, a test-time mechanism that generates multiple candidate drafts from a single diffusion forward pass (exploiting parallel sampling from position-wise marginals) and uses the verifier to select the draft with highest expected throughput before applying lossless verification. Together, these methods achieve state-of-the-art speedups of up to 5.5× over standard decoding by simultaneously reducing draft latency through parallelism and improving drafter-verifier alignment through theoretically-motivated training and inference procedures.

title: Chain of Though Empowers Transformers to Solve Inherently Serial Problems
author: Zhiyuan Li et al
description: This paper investigates why chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting improves the reasoning capabilities of decoder-only transformers through the lens of computational expressiveness. The authors prove that constant-depth transformers with constant-bit precision can only solve AC⁰ problems without CoT, but with T steps of CoT and O(log n) embedding size, they can solve any problem computable by boolean circuits of size T. They demonstrate this theoretically by showing CoT allows transformers to perform inherently serial computations that bypass the depth limitations, essentially using each CoT step to simulate one gate operation in a target circuit. Empirically, they validate these findings on four tasks (modular addition, permutation composition, iterated squaring, and circuit value problems), showing that CoT dramatically improves accuracy on problems requiring serial computation, especially for shallow transformers.

title: d3LLM: Ultra-Fast dLLM using Pseudo-Trajectory Distillation
author: Yu-Yang Qian et al
description: This paper introduces d3LLM, a framework that balances accuracy and parallelism in diffusion language models through innovations in both training and inference. During training, they propose pseudo-trajectory distillation, which uses the teacher model's own decoding trajectory (even when it differs from ground truth) to teach the student which tokens can be confidently decoded earlier, combined with curriculum learning that progressively increases mask ratios and window sizes. During inference, they introduce entropy-based multi-block decoding that simultaneously decodes across multiple blocks by prioritizing low-entropy (high-confidence) tokens, coupled with a KV-cache refresh mechanism that periodically recomputes cached states to prevent quality degradation from error propagation. They also propose AUP (Accuracy Under Parallelism), a new evaluation metric that measures the weighted area under the accuracy-parallelism curve, penalizing methods that sacrifice accuracy for speed while rewarding those that maintain both.

title: Fast-dLLM v2: Efficient Block-Diffusion LLM
author: Chengyue Wu et al
description: This paper presents Fast-dLLM v2, which achieves 2.5× speedup over autoregressive LLMs by dividing text generation into blocks that use diffusion-based parallel decoding. The key technique is a block diffusion mechanism where tokens within each block can be predicted and refined simultaneously through bidirectional attention, while blocks themselves are generated sequentially using cached autoregressive dependencies. During inference, the model employs confidence-based parallel decoding that finalizes multiple high-confidence tokens simultaneously within a block, avoiding the sequential one-token-at-a-time bottleneck of traditional models. A hierarchical caching system further accelerates generation by storing completed block representations (block-level cache) and maintaining separate prefix/suffix caches for partially decoded blocks (DualCache), enabling efficient parallel refinement without redundant computation.

title: Diffusion Language Models Know the Answer Before Decoding
author: Pengxiang Li et al
description: Prophet accelerates diffusion language models by exploiting "early answer convergence"—the observation that correct answers often emerge as top-1 predictions at intermediate steps, with up to 99% of instances converging by halfway through decoding. The method monitors the confidence gap (difference between top-2 logits) and uses adaptive thresholds that become more permissive as decoding progresses, allowing the model to commit all remaining tokens and terminate early when predictions stabilize. Experiments on LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B show 1.2-3.4× speedups across diverse tasks while maintaining accuracy within ±1%, demonstrating that the method automatically allocates more steps to complex problems and fewer to simpler ones. This reframes DLM decoding as an optimal stopping problem and provides a training-free, plug-and-play acceleration technique complementary to existing optimization methods.

title: The Flexibility Trap: Why Arbitrary Order Limits Reasoning Potential in Diffusion Language Models
author: Zanlin Ni et al
description: This paper reveals "The Flexibility Trap"—a counter-intuitive finding that diffusion language models' ability to generate tokens in arbitrary order actually narrows rather than expands their reasoning capabilities, as measured by Pass@k metrics showing autoregressive (left-to-right) decoding achieves higher solution space coverage. The authors identify "entropy degradation" as the mechanism: arbitrary-order models bypass high-uncertainty "forking tokens" (logical connectives like "Therefore") that represent critical reasoning branch points, prematurely collapsing the solution space by resolving easier tokens first and then filling in connectors to match pre-generated conclusions rather than genuinely exploring logical paths. Based on this insight, they propose JustGRPO, which simply applies standard Group Relative Policy Optimization by treating diffusion models as autoregressive during training (eliminating the complexity of handling combinatorial trajectory spaces and intractable likelihoods), achieving state-of-the-art results like 89.1% on GSM8K while fully preserving parallel decoding at inference time. The work challenges the assumption that arbitrary-order generation is beneficial for reasoning, demonstrating that the autoregressive constraint acts as an effective training scaffold that forces confrontation with uncertainty at logical forks, thereby maintaining broader reasoning potential.

title: How Many Samples are Needed to Train a Deep Neural Network?
author: Pega Golestaneh et al
description: This paper establishes a fundamental limit on how many training samples are needed for deep ReLU neural networks, proving that the generalization error decreases at a rate of 1/√n rather than the faster 1/n rate seen in classical methods like linear regression. Using information theory (Fano's inequality), the authors prove that achieving a prediction error of ε² requires approximately 1/ε⁴ training samples—significantly more than the 1/ε² samples needed by traditional statistical methods. Their theoretical lower bound matches existing upper bounds and shows the error depends logarithmically on input dimension and involves a sparsity factor based on ℓ1-norm constraints on network weights. Extensive experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR10, and regression datasets confirm this 1/√n scaling holds in practice for both feedforward networks and CNNs, providing rigorous mathematical support for practitioners' belief that deep learning requires "many" training samples.

title: The Crystal Ball Hypothesis in Diffusion Models: Anticipating Object Positions from Initial Noise
author: Yuanhao Ban et al
description: This paper discovers "trigger patches"—specific regions within the initial Gaussian noise of diffusion models that consistently determine where objects will appear in generated images, regardless of the text prompt. The authors develop both a posterior analysis method (measuring "trigger entropy" based on object position variance) and train a detector that can identify these patches directly from noise with 0.325 mAP50, demonstrating that trigger patches are statistical outliers in the Gaussian distribution rather than random noise. Through two-sample tests and experiments with handcrafted patches (shifted Gaussians and sinusoidal signals), they confirm that patches deviating from standard Gaussian distributions are more effective triggers, with injection success rates reaching 81% for certain synthetic patches. The findings enable two practical applications: removing trigger patches to increase positional diversity of generated objects (achieving entropy of 171.84 vs 135.97 baseline), and strategically placing trigger patches to improve prompt adherence for positional instructions (boosting success rate from 57% to 84%).

title: WSM: Decay-Free Learning Rate Schedule via Checkpoint Merging for LLM Pre-training
author: Changxin Tian et al
topic: llm, schedule, training, learning-rate, lr, lr-schedule
description: WSM (Warmup-Stable and Merge) is a decay-free learning rate scheduling framework that replaces the traditional decay phase with checkpoint merging, establishing a formal theoretical connection between the two: merging checkpoints with specific weights is mathematically equivalent to applying a synthetic decay schedule to accumulated gradients. This theoretical foundation allows WSM to emulate any decay strategy (cosine, linear, inverse square root) as a principled model averaging scheme, without modifying the live training process. Through extensive experiments, the authors identify merge duration—the training window covered by merged checkpoints—as the single most critical hyperparameter, more impactful than checkpoint interval or number of merged models. WSM consistently outperforms the widely-used WSD baseline, with gains of +3.5% on MATH, +2.9% on HumanEval, and +5.5% on MMLU-Pro, with benefits extending to supervised fine-tuning as well.

title: Enabling Approximate Joint Sampling in Diffusion LMs
author: Parikshit Bansal et al
topic: sampling, dlm, diffusion, llm, marginal, joint, kl-divergence
description: This paper identifies that parallel token generation in masked diffusion language models is fundamentally flawed because it samples from the product of independent marginal distributions rather than the true joint distribution the model encodes, causing well-documented accuracy drops at higher parallelism. The main contribution is ADJUST, a lightweight single-layer transformer trained on top of a frozen diffusion model that enables approximate joint sampling: after one full model forward pass, ADJUST runs K−1 additional cheap forward passes, each conditioning on previously sampled tokens to sequentially unmask multiple tokens in a dependency-aware manner. The authors establish a formal probabilistic framework connecting parallel sampling's failure to marginal-vs-joint distribution mismatch, and develop a specialized training methodology using the base model's own denoising trajectories as training data, minimizing KL divergence between ADJUST's output and true joint sampling. Empirically, ADJUST achieves dramatic improvements over naive parallel decoding—for example, +16 percentage points on GSM8K and +0.5 MAUVE at K=4—while adding only 20–25% latency overhead compared to naive parallel sampling, since ADJUST has just 200M parameters versus 7B for the base model.

title: Residual Context Diffusion Language Models
author: Yuezhoe Hu et al
description: This paper identifies that block-wise diffusion LLMs waste computation during inference because the remasking step discards low-confidence token representations that actually contain useful contextual information, as evidenced by high token recall in early denoising steps. The core contribution is Residual Context Diffusion (RCD), which recycles these discarded representations by converting them into entropy-weighted residual vectors—using normalized Shannon entropy to weight tokens by their uncertainty—and injecting them as soft token embeddings into the input of the next denoising step. To avoid the prohibitive memory cost of backpropagation-through-time through this recursive loop, the authors propose a decoupled two-stage training pipeline where a frozen lightweight reference model generates stable proxy residuals to supervise a separate trainable target model, along with a temperature-scaled entropy alignment mechanism to bridge the train-test distribution gap. RCD consistently improves frontier dLLMs by 5–10 accuracy points across benchmarks, nearly doubles accuracy on AIME24/25, and achieves 4–5× fewer denoising steps at equivalent accuracy, all while adding minimal computational overhead and converging efficiently with only ~1B training tokens.

title: One Token is Enough: Improving Diffusion Language Models with a Sink Token
author: Zihou Zhang et al
description: This paper identifies the "moving sink" phenomenon in diffusion language models: unlike autoregressive models where causal masking naturally anchors attention sinks to the fixed initial token, DLMs lack this structural constraint, causing sink positions to shift erratically across denoising steps as the masked token set changes dynamically. Through value-space analysis, the authors show that sink tokens in DLMs consistently exhibit significantly lower L2 norms than other tokens, functioning as an implicit protective mechanism against excessive information mixing—but their instability undermines inference robustness. The proposed solution is remarkably minimal: prepend a single extra [SINK] token constrained by a modified attention mask to attend only to itself while remaining globally visible, providing a stable, semantically neutral anchor that the model learns to use for attention offloading. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness is position-invariant (front or end of sequence performs similarly), saturates at a single token (adding more yields negligible gains), and holds even when the sink token's value states are forced to zero vectors—validating that low-norm structure rather than semantic content is the operative mechanism, with consistent benchmark improvements across both AR-initialized and scratch-trained DLMs.

title: Post-LayerNorm is Back: Stable, Expressive, and Deep
author: Chen Chen et al
description: The paper identifies that Post-LayerNorm (Post-LN) instability in deep networks stems from ResNet-style residual connections causing gradient vanishing, not from normalization itself. To address this, the authors introduce Keel, which replaces the ResNet residual path with a Highway-style gated connection scaled by α = L (number of layers), preserving gradient flow through deep networks without requiring specialized initialization. They provide formal gradient flow analysis proving that Keel's modification maintains consistent gradient magnitudes across depths, enabling training stability at extreme depths (1000+ layers). Empirically, the method demonstrates superior performance across all depths with +10 point improvements on reasoning tasks like GSM-8K at 1024 layers, while maintaining stability at 50% higher learning rates than Pre-LN baselines.

title: GDPO: Group reward-Decoupled Normalization Policy Optimization for Multi-reward RL Optimization
author: Shih-Yang Liu et al
topic: multi-reward rl, policy optimization, grpo, reward normalization, rl
description: The paper identifies that directly applying Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to multi-reward reinforcement learning causes distinct reward combinations to collapse into identical advantage values, which diminishes the precision of the training signal and leads to suboptimal convergence. To address this problem, the authors propose Group reward-Decoupled Normalization Policy Optimization (GDPO), which normalizes each individual reward separately before aggregation, then applies batch-wise advantage normalization to preserve cross-reward distinctions while maintaining stable numerical ranges. The method is systematically evaluated across three diverse tasks—tool calling, mathematical reasoning, and coding reasoning—each optimizing multiple heterogeneous rewards including correctness, format compliance, length constraints, and code quality. Empirical results demonstrate that GDPO consistently outperforms GRPO across all settings, with particularly significant improvements on complex reasoning tasks (e.g., 6.3% accuracy gain on AIME for DeepSeek-R1-1.5B) while maintaining better adherence to constraints.

title: Muon is Scalable for LLM Training
author: Jingyuan Liu et al
topic: optimizer, muon, matrix orthogonalization, llm training
description: The paper demonstrates that Muon, a matrix orthogonalization-based optimizer, can be effectively scaled to large language models through two key techniques: incorporating weight decay and carefully adjusting per-parameter update scales to maintain consistent update magnitudes across different matrix shapes. The authors develop a distributed implementation of Muon compatible with ZeRO-1 that achieves optimal memory efficiency and reduced communication overhead while preserving the algorithm's mathematical properties. Scaling law experiments show that Muon achieves approximately 2× computational efficiency compared to AdamW under compute-optimal training, requiring only 52% of the FLOPs to match AdamW's performance. The authors introduce Moonlight, a 3B/16B-parameter Mixture-of-Expert model trained with 5.7T tokens using Muon, which advances the Pareto frontier of model performance versus training FLOPs and demonstrates particularly strong improvements on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks.

title: Efficient Training of Language Models to Fill in the Middle
author: Mohammad Bavarian et al
description: The paper demonstrates that autoregressive language models can efficiently learn fill-in-the-middle (FIM) infilling capabilities by applying a straightforward data transformation that moves a middle span of text to the end of documents, without compromising left-to-right generative performance—a phenomenon termed the "FIM-for-free" property. Extensive scaling studies across code and natural language domains show that models trained with up to 90% FIM rate maintain identical autoregressive test loss and downstream benchmark performance as baseline models, while simultaneously learning robust infilling capabilities. Through comprehensive ablations on key hyperparameters, the authors identify optimal practices including context-level FIM application, character-level span selection, and joint SPM+PSM training modes, with SPM showing slight performance advantages due to improved key-value caching during inference. In contrast to pretraining efficiency, the paper reveals that learning FIM through finetuning requires substantial additional compute (50B tokens at 90% FIM rate with high learning rates), suggesting fundamental differences in how models acquire this capability at different training stages.

title: The Road Less Scheduled
author: Aaron Defazio et al
topic: optimization, learning rate schedules, sgd, iterate averaging
description: This paper introduces Schedule-Free learning, an optimization method that eliminates the need to pre-specify a stopping time T while achieving state-of-the-art performance compared to traditional learning rate schedules. The key innovation is a new form of momentum based on interpolation between sequences (z for base updates and x for averaging) that unifies iterate averaging with scheduling theory. The authors develop a novel online-to-batch conversion theorem (Theorem 2) that explains why their approach works and generalizes several existing optimization techniques. Extensive experiments across 28 diverse problems—from convex optimization to large-scale deep learning—demonstrate that Schedule-Free AdamW matches or exceeds heavily-tuned cosine schedules without introducing additional hyperparameters, and the method won Meta's entry to the MLCommons 2024 AlgoPerf challenge.

title: A Closer Look at Deep Learning Heuristics: Learning Rate Restarts, Warmup, and Distillation
author: Akhilesh Gotmare et al
topic: deep learning optimization, learning rate schedules, knowledge distillation
description: This paper empirically analyzes three common deep learning heuristics—cosine annealing with restarts (SGDR), learning rate warmup, and knowledge distillation—using mode connectivity and canonical correlation analysis (CCA) to understand their mechanisms. The authors find that the conventional explanation for cosine annealing's success (escaping local minima) is oversimplified, as iterates move over loss barriers but don't necessarily escape distinct minima; learning rate warmup primarily prevents instability in deeper layers during large-batch training, an effect that can be replicated by freezing those layers; and knowledge distillation primarily transfers dark knowledge to deeper (discriminative) layers rather than shallow feature-extraction layers. This work advances understanding of deep learning training dynamics through empirical analysis tools rather than relying on linear interpolation or intuitive explanations.

title: Scaling Laws and Compute-Optimal Training Beyong Fixed Training Durations
author: Alexander Hagele et al
topic: learning rate schedules, language model training, scaling laws
description: This paper challenges the ubiquitous use of cosine learning rate schedules in large language model training by demonstrating that a simpler alternative—constant learning rate followed by a short cooldown phase—achieves comparable performance without requiring the training duration to be specified in advance. The authors show that a (1-sqrt) cooldown function outperforms linear decay, and that stochastic weight averaging can further improve performance along the training trajectory. Critically, they demonstrate that this approach enables significantly cheaper scaling law experiments by requiring only a single long training run per model size followed by retrospective cooldowns at desired checkpoints, reducing compute costs by approximately half compared to the standard practice of retraining models from scratch with length-matched cosine schedules.

title: Training trajectories, mini-batch losses and the curious role of the learning rate
author: Mark Sandler et al
topic: sgd, learning rate schedules, weight averaging
description: This paper investigates the curious behavior of stochastic gradient descent by analyzing how the loss function behaves differently on fixed training batches versus held-out batches, revealing that loss on a training batch can reach remarkably low values in a single gradient step while loss on unseen batches shows opposite behavior. The authors propose a simple quadratic model of the loss landscape that explains key phenomena and demonstrates a novel equivalence: weight averaging techniques (stochastic weight averaging, exponential moving average, and two-point averaging) are mathematically equivalent to specific learning rate schedules. This theoretical insight connects to practical findings showing that averaging improves performance by moving trajectories inside loss basins, and helps explain why weight averaging and learning rate decay achieve similar effects in training dynamics.

title: Self-Speculative Masked Diffusions
author: Andrew Campbell et al
description: Self-Speculative Masked Diffusions proposes a new class of masked diffusion models (MDMs) that reduces the number of neural network forward passes needed for high-quality discrete data generation. The core contribution is a hybrid transformer architecture combining non-causal layers (for draft token generation) with a small causal layer (following the σ-GPT design), enabling speculative sampling entirely within a single model without needing a separate draft network. By using the non-causal factorized distribution as a draft and the causal autoregressive distribution as a target, the method produces non-factorized predictions over masked positions, achieving roughly 2× fewer function evaluations on text and protein sequence benchmarks. The authors also provide theoretical analysis characterizing the output distribution of their sampling algorithm and derive a log-likelihood lower bound for this model class. Key difference from TiDAR: Both papers combine diffusion and autoregressive mechanisms in a single model, but their goals and orientations differ significantly. Self-Speculative Masked Diffusions is built on top of MDMs — it targets any-order generation (not left-to-right) and is particularly suited for domains like protein sequences where no natural ordering exists. TiDAR, by contrast, is fundamentally AR-first: it uses diffusion only as a parallelizable drafting mechanism while the final sampling is strictly left-to-right autoregressive, preserving AR-quality guarantees. TiDAR also explicitly targets wall-clock throughput speedups (4-6×) and KV-cache compatibility, whereas the MDM paper focuses on reducing NFEs in a compute-agnostic sense without claiming AR-level quality parity.

title: Diffusion-Pretrained Dense and Contextual Embeddings
author: Sedigheh Eslami et al
description: pplx-embed introduces a family of multilingual embedding models built on diffusion-pretrained language model backbones, converting causal decoder-only LLMs (Qwen3) into bidirectional encoders via continued diffusion pretraining to better capture global document context. The key technical contributions are a multi-stage contrastive training pipeline (pair → contextual → triplet training with model merging) and a novel quantization-aware mean pooling strategy that natively outputs INT8 and binary embeddings, dramatically improving storage efficiency (up to 4× over comparable models). They release two model variants — pplx-embed-v1 for standard retrieval and pplx-embed-context-v1 for chunk-level contextual retrieval using late chunking — at both 0.6B and 4B scales, with the contextual variant achieving state-of-the-art on ConTEB (81.96% nDCG@10), surpassing voyage-context-3 and Anthropic Contextual. Evaluation on internal web-scale benchmarks (up to 1B production web pages) further validates real-world deployment effectiveness, with pplx-embed-v1-0.6B notably outperforming the larger Qwen3-Embedding-4B on several tasks.

title: Breaking the Factorization Barrier in Diffusion Language Models
author: Ian Li et al
description: CoDD identifies that the core limitation in diffusion language models isn't backbone capacity but a structural misspecification: forcing the output distribution to be fully factorized makes parallel token prediction inherently incoherent, since the model cannot capture how simultaneously predicted tokens depend on each other. The key technical insight is that replacing this factorized output with a product of the neural network's logits and a learned Probabilistic Circuit (PC) yields a distribution that is both expressive enough to capture complex inter-token dependencies and compact enough to avoid exponential parameter scaling. The PC's decomposability property makes the otherwise intractable partition function computable in a single feedforward pass, and because the Transformer backbone can be frozen, the PC is trained as a lightweight plug-in requiring only ~3 GPU hours. Empirically, this approach consistently improves reasoning benchmarks across architectures and is especially impactful in low-step generation regimes, recovering GSM8K accuracy from 34% to 56.4% at 64 steps while adding less than 6% inference latency overhead.

title: SPG: Sandwiched Policy Gradient for Masked Diffusion Language Models
author: Chenyu Wang et al
description: Applying RL to diffusion language models is complicated by the fact that the true log-likelihood is intractable, and prior work's use of the ELBO as a surrogate introduces asymmetric bias — it's a lower bound, so minimizing it for bad outputs doesn't reliably reduce their true likelihood. SPG resolves this by "sandwiching" the log-likelihood: maximizing the ELBO for positive-reward outputs while minimizing a tractable Evidence Upper Bound (EUBO), derived from a Rényi variational bound, for negative-reward ones, making the overall policy gradient a valid proxy for the true objective in both directions. In practice, the negative-reward term uses a mixture of ELBO and EUBO rather than EUBO alone, which is shown both theoretically and empirically to reduce gradient variance and stabilize training. SPG also introduces a block-wise masking strategy for Monte Carlo estimation that aligns the training distribution with the semi-autoregressive inference strategy used at generation time, yielding especially large gains on structured reasoning tasks like Countdown (+18.4%) and Sudoku (+27%) over prior state-of-the-art RL methods.

title: Late Chunking: Contextual Chunk Embeddings Using Long-Context Embedding Models
author: Michael Gunther et al
description: Standard RAG chunking loses contextual information because chunks are embedded independently, meaning pronouns and references like "its" or "the city" get separated from the entities they refer to, degrading retrieval quality. Late chunking fixes this by running the full document through the transformer first to get contextualized token embeddings, and only then applying mean pooling over chunk-boundary segments — so each chunk embedding inherits context from the entire document without any modification to the model itself. For documents exceeding the model's context window, the paper extends this with "long late chunking," which processes overlapping macro-chunks and stitches together the resulting token embeddings before pooling. They also propose a "span pooling" fine-tuning method that trains the model to specifically encode relevant span information into token embeddings, yielding modest additional gains, and show the approach matches LLM-based contextual embedding methods (like Anthropic's Claude-based chunk augmentation) at a fraction of the computational cost.
